preFix.title: Making a small timber step stool
preFix.subtitle: Build a two-step stool from PAR timber with through-tenon or screwed joints
preFix.excerpt: How to make a small two-step stool from 25 mm PAR timber, covering cutting the side pieces with the stepped profile, fitting the tread pieces, and finishing with rounded edges and a paint or wax coat.
preFix.sourceNotes: Carpentry for Boys (1914). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery.

content.text: A small two-step stool is a practical first joinery project. It uses only hand tools and a cordless drill. The key is cutting the side pieces accurately: they carry all the load and their profile defines the shape of the whole stool. A
where the tread slots into a groove in the side gives a stronger result than a simple butt joint. Both methods are described here.

From 25 mm PAR timber (actual dimension 21 to 22 mm): two side pieces at 450 x 300 mm, two tread pieces at 250 x 250 mm, and a rear stretcher at 250 x 100 mm for bracing across the back. No. 8 x 50 mm screws, PVA glue. Wax or hard-wearing floor paint for the finish.
Step 1, mark out the side pieces

Mark the stepped profile on both side pieces identically. The lower step sits 200 mm above the floor; the upper step sits at 400 mm (the finished heights depend on the intended use, but a 200 mm rise per step gives a comfortable ascent). Mark the tread positions as horizontal lines at these heights. Each tread extends 200 mm deep at the outer face, tapering back to zero at the rear. The back of each step aligns vertically above the back of the step below.
Step 2, cut the housing grooves in the side pieces

Mark each housing groove 6 mm deep at the tread positions on the inner face of each side piece. Cut the two sides of the groove with the tenon saw (held against a square to cut straight down), then remove the waste with the chisel. Check the groove is 6 mm deep across its full width and that the base is flat. Cut both sides to match before assembly: the treads must seat at the same height on both sides.

Round the front corner of each tread with the block plane and the sanding block: a 3 to 4 mm radius is enough to remove the sharp corner without looking overly heavy. Round the front curved edge of each side piece in the same way. Work from coarse (80 grit) to fine (180 grit). Sharp corners on a step stool cause unnecessary bruising when the stool is knocked against a shin.

Apply PVA into the housing grooves and on the tread ends. Slide the treads into the grooves and cramp the assembly. Pre-drill and drive one No. 8 x 50 mm screw through each side piece into each tread end from the outside face. Fit the rear stretcher across the back of both side pieces at the lower level, glued and screwed. The stretcher prevents the sides from splaying under load. Allow the glue to dry for 24 hours before use.

Fill any screw heads and gaps with wood filler, sand smooth when dry, and apply a finish. A hard-wearing floor paint gives a durable, cleanable surface for a painted kitchen stool. Clear wax or hard wax oil works well on a natural timber finish. The tread surfaces need more protection than the sides: apply two coats to the treads.

A single-step stool with a lower platform height (150 to 200 mm) makes a useful bathroom stepping stool for children. A three-step version follows the same method with an additional tread at 600 mm height. Keep each tread at the same 200 mm depth; the overall footprint grows with each additional step.

Check the joint screws annually: the housing joint is the primary structural connection and screws in end grain can work loose with use. Re-drive any loose screw with a slightly longer replacement. Repaint the tread surfaces when they begin to show wear.

attrs.fix: Stand the stool on a flat surface and mark the high corner. Plane or sand the high corner down until all four feet sit flat.
attrs.cause: The two feet of one side piece are not in the same plane as the other; one corner is slightly longer.
attrs.symptom: Stool rocks on the floor after assembly

attrs.fix: Pare the groove sides with the chisel, taking very light cuts, until the tread fits without force. The tread should slide in with hand pressure and feel snug, not require a mallet.
attrs.cause: The groove was cut slightly too narrow; timber varies slightly in thickness.
attrs.symptom: Housing groove is too tight and the tread does not seat fully

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
